result.chapter_summary: The author argues that persistent educational frustrations in the U.S. stem from a devotion to flawed theories—naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism—rather than a lack of resources or effort. He proposes a shift from child-centeredness to community-centeredness, citing new cognitive science and the historical 'natural experiment' of France's 1989 educational reform as evidence that communal, knowledge-rich schooling is the primary path to social equality.

claims.text: Six well-recognized educational frustrations in the United States (over-testing, preschool fade-out, narrowed curriculum, low verbal scores, achievement gaps, and Common Core tribulations) are caused by faulty ideas.
claims.source_passage: These problems have defied solution—but not primarily from lack of will or money, or from poverty or the shortcomings of teachers. They resist solution because they cannot be solved under the reign of the faulty ideas that caused them to arise.

claims.text: The most effective form of educational reform is a change in the dominant ideas guiding the system.
claims.source_passage: My long-standing view is that idea change will be the most effective educational reform of all.

claims.text: The achievement gap is primarily a knowledge gap and a language gap.
claims.source_passage: the path-breaking work of researchers like John Guthrie, Betty Hart, and Todd Risley should have made it unnecessary to assert once more at this late date that the achievement gap is chiefly a knowledge gap and a language gap.

claims.text: Knowledge-based schooling can significantly ameliorate the achievement gap.
claims.source_passage: It can be greatly ameliorated by knowledge-based schooling.

claims.text: Parity of knowledge among young pupils should be recognized as a civil right.
claims.source_passage: the right to parity of knowledge among young pupils will come to be understood as a civil right.

claims.text: The possibility of success for all children requires systematically imparting the knowledge commonly possessed by successful citizens.
claims.source_passage: only by systematically imparting to all children the knowledge that is commonly possessed by successful citizens can all children gain the possibility of success

claims.text: Success consists of autonomy, commanding respect, having a communal voice, earning a living, and contributing to the community.
claims.source_passage: “success” understood as becoming a person with autonomy, who commands respect, has a communal voice that can write and speak effectively to strangers, can earn a good living, and can contribute to the wider community.

claims.text: Elementary schools must shift focus from self-realization to community-centeredness.
claims.source_passage: the key task facing our elementary schools is to shift our emphasis from the goal of self-­realization to the goal of community—from child-centeredness to community-centeredness.

claims.text: Dominant educational theories unwittingly destroy the American dream.
claims.source_passage: I am still chiefly motivated by the social injustice of our dominant theories and their unwitting destruction of the American dream.

claims.text: The national mood has changed to a point where heterodox ideas about knowledge-based education may receive a new hearing.
claims.source_passage: The context and the national mood have changed. Heterodox ideas that were rejected a few years ago might now be granted a new hearing after the frustrations of current reform efforts.

claims.text: The French elementary school system was historically characterized by egalitarian uniformity through a national curriculum.
claims.source_passage: For many decades the French elementary school had been the pride and the terror of the young, with every child, rich or poor, having to undergo the very same rigors under the same national curriculum.

claims.text: The 1989 French education law (loi Jospin) radically transformed French schools by replacing the national curriculum with locally determined, individualized curriculums.
claims.source_passage: But in 1989, the bicentenary of the French Revolution, France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums

claims.text: The 1989 French educational reforms were the result of twenty years of teacher indoctrination in American-style progressive education within French education schools.
claims.source_passage: This drastic change had been silently prepared for during two decades of teacher indoctrination within French education schools into American-style progressive education.

claims.text: The term 'communal' is more appropriate than 'acculturative' to describe the goal of schooling because it emphasizes adding a shared social culture to a student's home culture.
claims.source_passage: In this book I use the adjective communal rather than acculturative to emphasize that schooling adds to home culture a communal culture that lends the ability to communicate with strangers.

claims.text: The 1989 French reforms prioritized student individuality, native abilities, interests, and home culture over a shared national body of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The new law reflected those ideas: more attention was to be paid to the individuality of each student, to his or her native abilities, interests, and home culture.

claims.text: The 1989 French reforms attempted to compensate for curriculum heterogeneity by emphasizing general skills like 'critical thinking' and 'learning to learn.'
claims.source_passage: To compensate for all this novel heterogeneity, the unifying emphasis was to be on general skills such as “critical thinking” and “learning to learn.”

claims.text: The implementation of the loi Jospin constituted a valid natural experiment because other key factors like teacher quality and budgets remained constant.
claims.source_passage: It was a natural experiment because many key elements of French education, other than curriculum, stayed constant over time. Teacher quality stayed the same by objective measures. School buildings and budgets did not change significantly.

claims.text: The shift from communal to individualized, skill-centered schooling in France caused an astonishingly steep decline in achievement across every demographic group.
claims.source_passage: They reported an astonishingly steep decline in achievement in each demographic group—children from the homes of wealthy executives and professionals, children from the middle classes, children from various other well-defined demographic groups including the unemployed

claims.text: The French shift to child-centered schooling disproportionately harmed the most economically disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Each group was academically harmed by the new system, and the harm became ever greater as one went down the economic scale. The children of the unemployed declined most of all.

claims.text: The increase in French educational inequality was a direct result of the 1989 curriculum and pedagogical changes.
claims.source_passage: Achievement decreased. Inequality increased dramatically.

claims.text: The decline in French test scores among the children of high-level professionals cannot be attributed to the influx of North African immigrants.
claims.source_passage: The massive declines that occurred at the very top among children of white-collar workers and high-level professionals and executives cannot be blamed on the influx of North African immigrants, as some American experts are inclined to say.

claims.text: American-style, individualistic educational theory yields far worse results for all students compared to community-centered models.
claims.source_passage: The American-style, individualistic theory yielded far worse results for every demographic group.

claims.text: French longitudinal studies provide a similar level of statistical confidence to the American NAEP studies despite different absolute sample sizes.
claims.source_passage: By those numbers alone, even without the refinements introduced by French and American experts, the two samplings yield very similar levels of confidence.

claims.text: Educational outcomes are largely controlled by the dominant pedagogical ideas in force rather than by irresistible social forces.
claims.source_passage: These historical facts, coupled with recent cognitive research, will add credibility to the view that our educational fate is largely controlled by ideas rather than by irresistible social forces.

claims.text: The decline in American student test scores in the 1970s was caused by the dominance of conceptions that began to take over public schools in the 1920s.
claims.source_passage: The decline in our student test scores in the 1970s was caused by the dominance of conceptions that had begun to take over American public schools starting in the 1920s.

claims.text: In the 1940s and 1950s, US public education scored near the top internationally in both achievement and equality.
claims.source_passage: As late as the 1940s and 1950s the public education of the United States, for all its racial and social shortcomings, scored near the top among nations in both achievement and equality.

claims.text: Between 1960 and 1980, American academic scores fell rapidly at all grade levels by more than 25 percent of a standard deviation.
claims.source_passage: But between 1960 and 1980 American academic scores fell rapidly at all grade levels—more than 25 percent of a standard deviation, a big drop for large populations.

claims.text: The test score decline between 1960 and 1980 occurred across all demographic groups.
claims.source_passage: As this book shows, the decline occurred at all grade levels among all demographic groups.

claims.text: The chief cause of the American educational decline was the nationwide adoption of naturalism, individualism, and skill-centrism.
claims.source_passage: The chief cause of the decline was the nationwide adoption of a set of inadequate ideas. ... they neglected the communal dimension of education in favor of individualistic child-centered development.

claims.text: Early education should be chiefly communal, focused on gaining proficiency in the language and conventions of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Early education should be chiefly communal—focused on gaining proficiency in the language and the conventions of the public sphere.

claims.text: If it is not harmful for an advantaged seven-year-old to know certain things, it cannot be developmentally inappropriate for a disadvantaged child to know those same things.
claims.source_passage: if an advantaged child at age seven knows certain things without harm, then it cannot be inherently harmful or “developmentally inappropriate” for a disadvantaged child also to know those very same things at the same age.

claims.text: Individuality can only be expressed through the mastery of shared social and linguistic conventions.
claims.source_passage: Elementary school is a time for building socialization as the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself. ... Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

claims.text: Early education should provide a uniform curriculum for every child in a given locality.
claims.source_passage: Every child in each locality should study basically the same early curriculum.

claims.text: Educational individualization should involve accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child rather than devising different curriculum topics.
claims.source_passage: there is a big distinction between accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan, and devising different curriculum topics for different children as we and the French now do in the child-centered school.

claims.text: Communalist schools accommodate shared curriculum topics for each child, whereas child-centered schools devise different curriculum topics for different children.
claims.source_passage: there is a big distinction between accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan, and devising different curriculum topics for different children as we and the French now do in the child-centered school.

claims.text: Elementary school is primarily a period for building socialization as a prerequisite for the later expression of individuality.
claims.source_passage: Elementary school is a time for building socialization as the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself.

claims.text: Children must master shared linguistic and social conventions, including national vocabulary and spelling, to enter the public sphere of the adult world.
claims.source_passage: Children need to master the shared conventions of the standard language and of social interaction. They need to learn the shared knowledge and vocabulary of the nation, the shared spelling, pronunciation, and other conventions in the public sphere of the grown-up world.

claims.text: Individuality is a product of full membership in the social community.
claims.source_passage: Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

claims.text: The naturalistic and individualistic view of education has paradoxically turned schools into soulless test-prep factories.
claims.source_passage: it is the naturalistic and individualistic view that has turned schools into soulless test-prep factories, with endless practice of strategies and skills

claims.text: The focus on test-prep and skill strategies is a desperate attempt to compensate for a lack of enabling knowledge induced by individualized curricula.
claims.source_passage: as they desperately attempt to overcome children’s lack of enabling knowledge—a lack partly induced by an individualized rather than a communal curriculum.

claims.text: Current individualized educational assumptions lead to instruction that is not child-centered and that perpetuates inequality.
claims.source_passage: our assumptions about how children learn have led to instruction that is far from child centered, and that perpetuates inequality among children from different backgrounds.

claims.text: Every child in a democracy deserves proficiency in the taken-for-granted knowledge of the standard language, regardless of home culture.
claims.source_passage: No matter what the home culture might be, every child deserves to become proficient in the taken-for-granted knowledge of the standard language.

claims.text: France possessed the highest achieving and most egalitarian school system in Europe from 1975 to 1985 due to its communal national curriculum.
claims.source_passage: No large nation has done so more successfully than France did from 1975 to 1985, when it had the highest achieving, most egalitarian school system of any large country in Europe.

claims.text: France’s educational decline after 1989 was caused by adopting the same child-centered guiding ideas that caused the American decline in the 1960s and 70s.
claims.source_passage: After 1989, the French in effect duplicated the American decline of the 1960s and 1970s by means of the same basic change in guiding ideas.

claims.text: Education without an explicit communal purpose is unlikely to achieve economic competence for all children.
claims.source_passage: Education without an explicit communal purpose is unlikely to achieve a communal result that offers every child economic competence and entrée into the public sphere.

claims.text: The educational ideas of naturalism and individualism contain a 'tincture of truth,' but skill-centrism is fundamentally problematic.
claims.source_passage: Any idea such as developmental appropriateness and child-centeredness that keeps earning the adherence of teachers all over the world must have a strong tincture of truth... But the third guiding idea, which one could call skill-centrism... is altogether problematic.

claims.text: Naturalism and individualism are rooted in a quasi-religious belief that nature is a manifestation of a benevolent God and therefore an unerring guide.
claims.source_passage: They arose from a belief that nature, as the earthly manifestation of a beneficent God, is unerring and benign. Hence the natural growth of a child is an instance of God unfolding His purposes in the world.

claims.text: In the United States, school districts historically maintained a 'common spirit' of communal purposiveness before the shift toward individualization.
claims.source_passage: the schools of most American districts did in earlier days have a strong communal purposiveness.

claims.text: Naturalism and individualism in education are linked by the belief that a child's natural growth is a manifestation of divine purpose.
claims.source_passage: Naturalism and individualism go together. They arose from a belief that nature, as the earthly manifestation of a beneficent God, is unerring and benign. Hence the natural growth of a child is an instance of God unfolding His purposes in the world.

claims.text: Providential individualism is the widely held faith that letting educational affairs take their natural course will yield optimal results due to a benevolent purpose in Nature.
claims.source_passage: I have adopted the phrase providential individualism to capture this point of view. I have found it useful in describing the widely held faith that if we let affairs take their natural course we are in the hands of a benign Providence, so all will be well, even optimal, in education.

claims.text: Loving and empathetic teaching is the best pedagogy for young children regardless of the underlying educational philosophy.
claims.source_passage: Of course, naturalism and individualism have no monopoly on a loving and empathetic teaching, which is in all cases the best pedagogy for young children.

claims.text: The belief that early education should be tailored to a child's individual abilities and temperament is a fatal weakness in American education.
claims.source_passage: Naturalism and individualism, beyond implying a loving pedagogy, have also been taken to imply—and this is a fatal weakness—a curriculum that arises from the child’s individual abilities and temperament.

claims.text: Equating early education with the metaphor of individual 'development' is misleading.
claims.source_passage: But I will argue, with support from developmental psychology, that equating early education with the metaphor of individual “development” is misleading

claims.text: Social impositions in schooling are natural occurrences rather than violations of a child's nature.
claims.source_passage: that so-called “unnatural” social impositions are the most natural things in the world

claims.text: School systems with lockstep curricula in early grades can be child-happy and highly effective.
claims.source_passage: that school systems with so-called “lockstep” curricula in the early grades (Finland, Japan) have very child-happy effective schools that score near the top in international studies.

claims.text: Differentiated curricula are harmful to both student achievement and social equity.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, international studies have shown that a differentiated curriculum is harmful to achievement and equity.

claims.text: There is weak to nonexistent scientific evidence supporting the existence of individual learning styles.
claims.source_passage: The evidence for individual learning styles is weak to nonexistent.

claims.text: Individualizing instruction in elementary classrooms leads to a fragmentation of the curriculum.
claims.source_passage: And in practice the individualizing of the elementary-school classroom has led to fragmentation of the curriculum.

claims.text: The goal of education as the imparting of content-independent general skills like critical thinking is a fallacy refuted by cognitive science.
claims.source_passage: This fragmentation is defended and supposedly turned to benefit by a third doctrine: that the goal of education is the imparting of general skills like critical thinking, creative thinking, problem solving, and cooperative thinking. But reality has not accepted this hopeful idea about skills, and recent cognitive science has been fatal to it.

claims.text: Educational individualism requires the general-skills idea to provide a sense of unity to a fragmented curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Educational individualism has always required the general-skills idea. To make thinking skill the ultimate goal renders irrelevant the fragmenting of school topics that must occur when the teacher is urged to tailor the curriculum to the uniqueness of each child.

claims.text: John Dewey proposed that scientific thinking could serve as a unifying factor to manage the distraction of individualized studies.
claims.source_passage: Unless these steps in advance are to end in distraction, some clue of unity, some principle that makes for simplification, must be found. This book represents the conviction that the needed steadying and centralizing factor is found in adopting as the end of endeavor that attitude of mind, that habit of thought, which we call scientific.

claims.text: Dewey's advocacy for critical thinking over factual content was a pragmatic response to the structural difficulty of individualized instruction.
claims.source_passage: Dewey is right about the structure of the difficulty... That can only be accomplished, Dewey says, by making critical thinking rather than mere facts the proper goal of child-centered education.

claims.text: When schooling is highly individualized, a further principle is required to guide instruction and provide unity to the student's experience.
claims.source_passage: if schooling is to be conducted as an 'individual experience' for each child, rather than 'in mass,' then some further principle is needed to guide instruction and lend unity to the experience of the individual student.

claims.text: Critical thinking, rather than facts, is the goal used to unify child-centered education.
claims.source_passage: That can only be accomplished, Dewey says, by making critical thinking rather than mere facts the proper goal of child-centered education.

claims.text: The emphasis on individualism in American education is a highly recalcitrant political problem because few are willing to challenge it.
claims.source_passage: he has also identified what may be the most recalcitrant political problem in American education—that few dare challenge our emphasis on individualism.

claims.text: Current educational theory relies on a scientifically incorrect view of skill development that assumes skills are general abilities.
claims.source_passage: The proposal that critical thinking is an aim that unifies fragmented and individualized schooling made sense in Dewey’s era, when scientists had incorrect ideas about skill development. But research on thinking skills is now a well-developed field, and its findings are fatal to this crucial refuge of current educational theory.

claims.text: Modern cognitive psychology rejects the view that general abilities like learning and problem solving can be studied independently of content domains.
claims.source_passage: Research clearly rejects the classical views on human cognition in which general abilities such as learning, reasoning, problem solving, and concept formation correspond to capacities and abilities that can be studied independently of the content domains.

claims.text: Educational skills are intrinsically tied to particular content domains, a principle known as domain specificity.
claims.source_passage: Modern cognitive psychology holds that the skills that are to be imparted to a child by the school are intrinsically tied to particular content domains. This is called the domain specificity of skills.

claims.text: Thinking skills cannot be readily transferred from one subject matter to another.
claims.source_passage: Thinking skills cannot readily be separated from one subject matter and applied to other subject matters.

claims.text: The term 'expertise' is a more accurate descriptor of educational goals than 'skill' because it implies specific domain knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Think of how significantly our view of schooling might change if suddenly policy makers, instead of using the term skill, had to use the more accurate, knowledge-drenched term expertise.

claims.text: A single, overarching skill that unifies learning does not exist.
claims.source_passage: Yet that single, overarching skill doesn’t exist. Believing in that mirage has actually resulted in the 'distraction' Dewey feared.

claims.text: The pursuit of nonexistent general skills has led schools to employ soul-deadening skill drills.
claims.source_passage: It has induced an ever-more-desperate effort to gain nonexistent skills through soul-deadening drills.

claims.text: Skill drills have failed to produce either proficient skills or high test scores.
claims.source_passage: These have produced neither good skills nor good scores on the ever-looming tests.

claims.text: Broad subject-matter knowledge is the fundamental basis of problem-solving abilities.
claims.source_passage: a fact which lends support to the idea that broad subject-matter knowledge is the basis of problem-solving abilities.

claims.text: The educational frustrations in the United States have not primarily been caused by a lack of will, lack of money, poverty, or the shortcomings of teachers.
claims.source_passage: These problems have defied solution—but not primarily from lack of will or money, or from poverty or the short-comings of teachers.

claims.text: A big influx of immigrants can reasonably be blamed for a decline in the overall average of national test scores, but not for a decline within the children of native-born elites.
claims.source_passage: It would certainly be reasonable to blame a big influx of immigrants for a decline in the average of French test scores. But a fair-minded person would hardly blame the children of immigrants... for a big decline among the children of native-born executives and professionals.

claims.text: American verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds have remained stagnant and low since 1971, indicating that the ideas that caused the initial decline are still in force.
claims.source_passage: The verbal scores of our seventeen-year-olds have stayed low and largely unchanged ever since NAEP began recording them in 1971. But by that year, the decline was already in full swing. The ideas that caused the decline still remain in full force today.

claims.text: Educational historians are correct that there was no literal 'golden age' of American education, despite the existence of a higher-scoring 'silver age.'
claims.source_passage: The belief that there was once a golden age of American education is scorned by educational historians. They are of course right.

claims.text: The achievement gap between Black and white students in the United States had narrowed steadily until recent decades.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

claims.text: Socialization and mastery of shared conventions in elementary school are the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself.
claims.source_passage: Elementary school is a time for building socialization as the only means through which individuality can ultimately express itself.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge Sequence is intended as only one possible exemplification of the communal knowledge principle, which can be realized through various curricula.
claims.source_passage: The Core Knowledge Sequence has always been offered as just one exemplification of the more general idea... which can be realized by different curricula that vary in interesting ways.

claims.text: The educational doctrines of naturalism and individualism are emotionally compelling because they align with parental love and solicitude for children.
claims.source_passage: This naturalism plus individualism is emotionally compelling. It is reinforced by our love and solicitude for young children.

claims.text: The goal of 'critical thinking' provides a necessary (though faulty) 'clue of unity' that prevents individualized instruction from descending into total distraction.
claims.source_passage: Unless these steps in advance are to end in distraction, some clue of unity, some principle that makes for simplification, must be found... the needed steadying and centralizing factor is found in adopting as the end of endeavor... that habit of thought, which we call scientific.

evidence.description: Studies demonstrating the correlation between knowledge, language, and the achievement gap.
evidence.source_reference: John Guthrie, Betty Hart, and Todd Risley

evidence.description: Historical documentation of the egalitarian uniform impulse in French education from the 18th to the 20th century.
The specific legislative change in France that shifted the system toward local, individualized curriculum.
Analysis of the intellectual drift in French teacher training schools preceding the 1989 reform.
evidence.source_reference: Philippe Nemo, Pourquoi Ont-ils Tué Jules Ferry? (1991); Liliane Lurçat, La Destruction de l’Enseignement Élémentaire (1998)

evidence.description: The loi Jospin (1989 French education law) which mandated a shift from national to local/individualized curriculum.
French Ministry of Education longitudinal studies analyzing twenty years of data on ten-year-olds following the 1989 reform.
Achievement data showing declines even among children of native-born French executives and professionals.
A comparison of the sample size of French longitudinal studies (4,200 out of 1 million) vs. NAEP studies (8,700 out of 6 million).
NAEP data showing low and unchanged verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds since 1971.
International studies of science achievement (1970-1984) showing US high ranking in the mid-century.
evidence.source_reference: J. P. Keeves, Learning Science in a Changing World (1992)

evidence.description: Data showing the narrowing of the black-white education gap until recent decades.
Standard deviation analysis of test score declines (25% overall, 50% for SAT verbal) between 1960 and 1980.
The French 'Crise de l’école' of 2007 as a parallel to the US 'Nation at Risk' decline.
The community-centered schools of Finland and Japan, which accommodate individual needs within a shared curriculum framework.
The French national curriculum system between 1975 and 1985, which produced high and egalitarian achievement.
The French educational decline post-1989 following the adoption of American-style progressive/child-centered ideas.
The Core Knowledge Sequence (1990) as a practical demonstration of how a communal curriculum can be structured.
Historical American elementary school curricula in history, geography, and civics that introduced children to shared cultural stories before the 'expanding environments' model.
International studies showing differentiated curricula correlate negatively with achievement and equity.
evidence.source_reference: Elliott A. Medrich, International Mathematics and Science Assessment (1992)

evidence.description: Cognitive science review showing that evidence for visual, auditory, and kinesthetic learning styles is weak to nonexistent.
Findings from expertise research indicating that thinking skills are not general but content-dependent.
evidence.source_reference: Feltovich et al., The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)

evidence.description: PISA scores showing students from nations with communal curricula (Japan, Finland) outperform American students in problem-solving.
A comprehensive handbook summarizing cognitive science research showing that cognition cannot be studied independently of content domains.
evidence.source_reference: Paul J. Feltovich, Michael J. Prietula, and K. Anders Ericsson, 'Studies of Expertise from Psychological Perspectives,' in The Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance (2006)
evidence.source_passage: Research clearly rejects the classical views on human cognition in which general abilities such as learning, reasoning, problem solving, and concept formation correspond to capacities and abilities that can be studied independently of the content domains.

evidence.description: Scientific claim that there is no such thing as developing a general skill.
evidence.source_reference: Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool, Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (2016)
evidence.source_passage: There is no such thing as developing a general skill.

evidence.description: International assessment data showing American 15-year-olds rank below nations with communal curricula in problem-solving.
evidence.source_reference: PISA 2012 Results: Creative Problem Solving Students’ Skills in Tackling Real-Life Problems (OECD, 2014)
evidence.source_passage: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades

concepts.author_definition: Becoming a person with autonomy, who commands respect, can speak effectively to strangers, earn a living, and contribute to the community.
concepts.common_alternative: Often defined narrowly in terms of grades, test scores, or individual wealth.
concepts.source_passage: “success” understood as becoming a person with autonomy, who commands respect, has a communal voice that can write and speak effectively to strangers, can earn a good living, and can contribute to the wider community.

concepts.author_definition: An educational focus on community goals and shared knowledge rather than individual self-realization.
concepts.common_alternative: Child-centeredness, which focuses on the individual child's natural unfolding.
concepts.source_passage: shift our emphasis from the goal of self-­realization to the goal of community—from child-centeredness to community-centeredness.

concepts.author_definition: A social culture added to the home culture by schools that enables communication with a wide national community and strangers.
concepts.common_alternative: Acculturative culture, which may imply replacing one's original home culture.
concepts.source_passage: In this book I use the adjective communal rather than acculturative to emphasize that schooling adds to home culture a communal culture that lends the ability to communicate with strangers.

concepts.author_definition: The 1989 French education law that ended the uniform national curriculum and introduced localized, individualized curricula ('projet').
A locally determined educational emphasis in French schools used to individualize instruction under the 1989 law.
An observational study where a policy change (like the loi Jospin) occurs in a way that allows for comparing outcomes while other variables remain constant.
The process of adopting American progressive educational ideas, specifically child-centeredness and skill-centrism, at the expense of communal knowledge.
The period of the 1940s and 1950s in American education characterized by high test scores and relative equality, prior to the decline of 1960-1980.
A concept often used to justify withholding academic content from children based on perceived natural development stages; Hirsch argues this is often applied inequitably to disadvantaged children.
concepts.common_alternative: The pedagogical practice of matching curriculum to a child's cognitive and emotional maturity level.

concepts.author_definition: An educational approach focused on mastering shared conventions, language, and knowledge of the public sphere to enable socialization and autonomy.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes caricatured as lock-step education or indoctrination.

concepts.author_definition: The idea that a 'public commons' of shared, enabling knowledge and language exists and that every child in a democracy should have access to it to effectively deploy national language and interact in the public sphere.
A widely held faith that letting affairs take their natural course in education leads to optimal results, based on the assumption that nature is guided by a benevolent purpose/Providence.
The educational aim of imparting general, content-independent skills like 'critical thinking' or 'problem solving' rather than specific domain knowledge.
The shared background knowledge and vocabulary possessed by successful adults that allows a person to participate in the public sphere and communicate with strangers.
A uniform, grade-by-grade academic sequence followed by all students in a school system, often criticized by progressives as 'unnatural.'
Critiqued by Hirsch in its root meaning of 'unfolding,' used to justify a belief that education should wait for a child's inherent nature to reveal itself.
The principle that thinking skills are intrinsically tied to specific subject-matter knowledge and cannot be easily transferred across domains.
concepts.common_alternative: The belief in 'general skills' or all-purpose cognitive abilities.
concepts.source_passage: Modern cognitive psychology holds that the skills that are to be imparted to a child by the school are intrinsically tied to particular content domains. This is called the domain specificity of skills.

concepts.author_definition: A state of proficiency that represents an inherent fusion of form and content, where knowledge and skill cannot be dissociated.
concepts.source_passage: expertise implies an inherent fusion of form and content, knowledge and skill that can’t easily be dissociated in the human mind.

concepts.author_definition: Repetitive instructional practices aimed at developing nonexistent general skills, which Hirsch characterizes as 'soul-deadening' and ineffective.
The ability to write and speak effectively to strangers and contribute to the wider community.
A de facto shared body of knowledge that enables a national language to be deployed effectively.
The phrase used in France to describe the academic achievement decline and increase in inequality following the 1989 education law.
An educational goal focused on the individual child's internal development, contrasted with community-centeredness.
The grown-up world where shared conventions of language, spelling, and vocabulary are taken for granted.

cases.name: The French Educational Transformation of 1989
cases.description: France moved from a highly centralized, uniform national curriculum established by Jules Ferry to a decentralized system under the loi Jospin. This shift followed 20 years of progressive pedagogical influence in teacher training schools. The new law emphasized student individuality and general skills like critical thinking, effectively 'Americanizing' the French system overnight.
key_passages: In 1989, the bicentenary of the French Revolution, France passed a radical new education law—the loi Jospin—requiring all elementary schools to cease teaching the national curriculum and begin teaching locally determined curriculums
This drastic change had been silently prepared for during two decades of teacher indoctrination within French education schools into American-style progressive education.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of teacher training as 'indoctrination'
The claim that the change was 'sudden and complete'
The negative impact of switching from a national to a local curriculum

cases.name: The French Education Reform (1989-present)
cases.description: In 1989, France abandoned its successful, community-centered national curriculum for a child-centered, skills-based model. Over the next twenty years, research showed massive declines in academic achievement for all social classes and a significant widening of the achievement gap. Hirsch uses this as a 'natural experiment' because external factors like teacher quality and budgets remained relatively stable during the transition.
key_passages: In 1989 the French decided to completely Americanize their school system overnight.

The broad new law enabled the Ministry of Education to conduct longitudinal studies comparing the effects of the communal elementary curriculum before 1989 with those of the individualized, skill-centered curriculums that followed.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of the 1989 law as 'Americanization'
The claim that teacher quality and other factors stayed constant
The causal link between pedagogical theory and the specific achievement declines

cases.name: France (post-1989)
cases.description: The French education system adopted American-style individualistic and child-centered ideas, resulting in a massive decline in student achievement comparable to the US decline of the 1970s.
key_passages: When the French later adopted those same ideas they suffered a decline of similar massive proportions. Our “Nation at Risk” of 1983 became their “Crise de l’école” of 2007.

cases.name: Finland and Japan
cases.description: Nations that maintain high performance and equity by utilizing community-centered schools where shared curriculum topics are accommodated for individual children rather than abandoned for individualized topics.
key_passages: accommodating shared curriculum topics for each child as the best schools do in the community-centered schools of Finland and Japan

cases.name: PISA 2012 Problem-Solving Rankings
cases.description: Comparison of problem-solving skills among 15-year-olds across different nations. American students scored significantly lower than students in Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland.
key_passages: The most recent PISA scores for the problem-solving abilities of fifteen-year-olds place American students well below Korea, Japan, Canada, and Finland, nations with set, communal curricula in early grades—a fact which lends support to the idea that broad subject-matter knowledge is the basis of problem-solving abilities.
contestable_aspects: Whether the correlation between communal curricula and PISA scores implies causation.
Whether PISA truly measures problem-solving independent of curriculum.

thinkers.name: Max Planck
thinkers.key_idea: Scientific truths triumph not by convincing opponents but because opponents die and a new generation grows up familiar with the truth.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Planck's observation to explain why entrenched educational theorists rarely change their minds and why he looks to the younger generation of scholars.
key_passages: He complained that professors never change their minds; they die off, and the younger generations take their places.

thinkers.name: Condorcet
thinkers.key_idea: The egalitarian impulse of educational uniformity.
thinkers.engagement: Cited as an early source of the French commitment to common education for all children.
key_passages: The egalitarian impulse of this uniformity was expressed early in the Revolution by Condorcet in his 1790 pamphlet A Common Education for Children

thinkers.name: Jules Ferry
thinkers.key_idea: Teachers should impart knowledge that is common and indispensable to all.
thinkers.engagement: Identified as the founder of modern French education whose principles were overturned in 1989.
key_passages: Ferry urged them to teach “that knowledge which is common to all and indispensable to all.”

thinkers.name: Giscard d’Estaing
thinkers.key_idea: Common knowledge across primary and middle schools is essential for reducing social inequality.
thinkers.engagement: Quoted to show that even late 20th-century French leaders maintained the focus on uniform knowledge for equity.
key_passages: stated, “The defining and acquiring of the very same knowledge by all French children... will be an essential element... in the reduction of inequalities of opportunity.”

thinkers.name: John Bishop
thinkers.key_idea: The narrowing of the educational gap between Black and White students until recent decades.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Bishop's historical data to demonstrate that the American system was capable of progress toward equality before the ideological shift took full effect.
key_passages: as John Bishop has long pointed out, the education gap between blacks and whites had narrowed steadily until recent decades.

thinkers.name: G. H. Mead
thinkers.key_idea: Individuality is a product of full membership in the 'tribe' or social group.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Mead's sociological theory to counter the claim that communal education stifles individuality.
key_passages: Only full membership in the tribe leads to individuality, as G. H. Mead profoundly observed.

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: Earlier American schooling had a common historical and cultural spirit prior to modern reforms.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Ravitch in the endnotes to support his claim that American education once possessed a communal purposiveness that has since been lost.
key_passages: Diane Ravitch, 'Tot Sociology: Or What Happened to History in the Grade Schools'

thinkers.name: A. O. Lovejoy
thinkers.key_idea: Tracing the historical connection between developmentalism and individualism.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Lovejoy with providing the 'influential and persuasive' history of how these two ideas became linked.
key_passages: The most influential and persuasive tracing of the connection between developmentalism and individualism has been A. O. Lovejoy

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: The scientific attitude of mind serves as the unifying goal of education to handle the distraction of individualized instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch treats Dewey not as a scapegoat but as an intellectual who correctly identified the structural need for a 'unifying principle' once education shifted to individualization.
key_passages: Dewey is right about the structure of the difficulty... he has also identified what may be the most recalcitrant political problem in American education—that few dare challenge our emphasis on individualism.

thinkers.name: Daniel Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: The lack of evidence for individual learning styles (visual, auditory, kinesthetic).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Willingham to provide scientific backing for his critique of educational individualization.
key_passages: The evidence for individual learning styles is weak to nonexistent.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: French minister associated with the 1989 law that decentralized the curriculum.

actors.name: John Guthrie
actors.context: Researcher whose work links achievement gaps to knowledge and language.

actors.name: Betty Hart
actors.context: Researcher whose work links achievement gaps to knowledge and language.

actors.name: Todd Risley
actors.context: Researcher whose work links achievement gaps to knowledge and language.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: The French Minister of Education associated with the 1989 law (loi Jospin).

actors.name: Ministry of Education (France)
actors.context: Conducted longitudinal studies on the effects of the 1989 educational reforms.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: The American body providing longitudinal achievement data used as a comparison for the French data.

actors.name: Educational Historians
actors.context: Hirsch notes that they scorn the idea of a 'golden age' but admits they concede the reality of the test score decline.

actors.name: Reagan Administration
actors.context: Convened the national commission that produced 'A Nation at Risk'.

actors.name: Knowledge Matters
actors.context: A new advocacy organization launched by experts to promote knowledge-rich curricula.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: Founded by Hirsch in 1986 to promote communal knowledge through the Core Knowledge Sequence.

actors.name: Teachers
actors.context: Adherents worldwide who are drawn to ideas like developmental appropriateness and child-centeredness.

actors.name: American school mission statements
actors.context: Cited as evidence of the institutionalized belief that education should be tailored to individual child uniqueness.

actors.name: K. Anders Ericsson
actors.context: Lead editor of the Cambridge Handbook of Expertise and Expert Performance, cited to debunk general skills theory.

actors.name: OECD
actors.context: Producer of PISA data used to compare international educational outcomes.

objections_raised.objection: The author hasn't changed his ideas for thirty years.
objections_raised.response: The basic themes regarding social injustice remain the same because the problems persist, but the author has gained greater clarity through new cognitive science findings.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in French test scores was caused by an influx of North African immigrants rather than the change in educational theory.
objections_raised.response: The decline was also massive among the children of wealthy, native-born French professionals and executives, which cannot be explained by immigration.

objections_raised.objection: The idea of a past 'golden age' of American education is a myth.
objections_raised.response: While historians are right to be skeptical of a 'golden age,' they nevertheless concede that a major, documented test-score decline of over 25% of a standard deviation occurred between 1960 and 1980.
Socialization and mastery of shared conventions are the only means through which true individuality and equality of opportunity can be expressed.

objections_raised.objection: The shift from individual to community curriculum implies a lack of affection or concern for the individual child.
objections_raised.response: On the contrary, the current individualistic assumptions perpetuate inequality; a communal focus is more child-centered because it provides the tools for real-world success.

objections_raised.objection: Uniform, communal curricula ('lockstep') are unnatural and make for unhappy schools.
objections_raised.response: International examples like Finland and Japan show that such schools are both 'child-happy' and highly effective.

objections_raised.objection: A fragmented curriculum is acceptable if the ultimate goal is teaching general critical thinking skills.
objections_raised.response: Cognitive science has proven that 'general skills' do not exist; skills are tied to domain-specific knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: If schooling is individualized, it becomes fragmented and lacks a unifying instructional principle.
objections_raised.response: Dewey attempted to solve this with 'critical thinking,' but since general skills don't exist, the only true way to lend unity and efficacy to education is through shared, specific content knowledge.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: this quick account is deliberately overschematized, since the Americanization process had been going on silently in teacher training for more than two decades.
I will use the details of the French results throughout this book, and devote chapter 7 to an analysis of the French experience.
The overwhelming evidence of a massive test score decline at all grade levels in all demographic groups between 1960 and 1980 is found in chapter 7.
Evidence of the massive test score decline at all grade levels is found in chapter 7.
Discussion of 'standard language' is found in chapter 6.
But I will show in chapter 1 that, paradoxically, it is the naturalistic and individualistic view that has turned schools into soulless test-prep factories
As I explain in chapter 7, this quick account [of France] is deliberately overschematized...
In chapter 8 I suggest that in the United States there be at least three different grade-by-grade sequences to choose from
Those distracting tests will be the subject of my first chapter.
